Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I'd study on certain days, but then I'd just... not. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when the anxiety got bad enough that I couldn't ignore it anymore. I'd spend maybe 12 hours a week total, but it was scattered and chaotic. I know it's not a good system, but organizing things feels overwhelming when you're already scared about everything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me to do anything. I'd go days without touching course material, then panic and cram when something was due. Other stuff in my life—work, just trying to get by with limited money—always seemed to push studying to the back burner. Regular schedules feel like a luxury I can't afford mentally or practically.
Q3: Fear, mostly. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered something was due or when the anxiety about falling behind got too intense. Sometimes I'd get a notification and feel this wave of dread, and that would push me to check. It wasn't curiosity or enthusiasm—it was more like checking to see how badly I was failing. I logged in pretty frequently, but it was always driven by worry rather than genuine interest.
Q4: There was a brief period where I tried to make it routine—like, checking in every morning with my coffee. It lasted maybe two weeks before everything fell apart. The mindfulness stuff they introduced actually helped a bit with the anxiety, which ironically made me less panicked about logging in constantly. But routine never really stuck. My life is too unpredictable for that kind of consistency.
Q5: The reading materials were most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace without the pressure of keeping up with a video. The discussion posts were surprisingly helpful too—seeing other people struggle with the same concepts made me feel less alone. I engaged with those more than I expected, even if my contributions weren't always deep.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the video content. Almost 300 minutes watched sounds like a lot, but I'd often have videos playing while doing other things, not really absorbing anything. The optional supplementary readings? Never touched them. When you're already drowning, anything labeled 'optional' feels like a lifeline you can skip. I just didn't have the mental energy for extras.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I couldn't see the immediate payoff, I'd skip it. Also, if something was clearly explained and didn't require me to hunt around for context, I was more likely to engage. My resources are limited, my time is limited, so I need things to be efficient. Anything that felt like busywork got ignored.
Q8: Optional stuff felt like a trap, honestly. Like, I knew it might help, but the anxiety of not completing required work made optional activities feel irresponsible. Required activities came with deadlines and consequences, which my anxious brain actually responds to. Optional things just sat there making me feel guilty for not doing them while I barely managed the mandatory stuff.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and work through them on my phone during bus rides or at work during slow periods. I couldn't always afford good internet at home, so offline was often my only option. I'd take handwritten notes in a cheap notebook because typing everything out wasn't practical. None of that shows up anywhere.
Q10: There were whole weeks where I was genuinely working through material but barely touched the LMS. I'd have a printed reading I was annotating, or I'd be reviewing my notes from discussions I'd already participated in. Sometimes I'd spend hours trying to understand a concept using the downloaded materials and free YouTube videos. The LMS would show nothing, but I was definitely learning.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a page, then never open the LMS version again. My internet isn't reliable, so I learned early to grab everything I might need in one session. I'd study from those downloaded files for days. The system probably thought I looked at something for five minutes when really I spent hours with it offline.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods. When my anxiety spiked, everything felt impossible. I'd open the LMS, see all the things I hadn't done, and just shut down completely. Financial stress made it worse—hard to focus on coursework when you're worried about rent. My resilience isn't great, so small setbacks would knock me out for days. The mindfulness exercises helped sometimes, but not always.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion was a big one too—if I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help. The fatigue was constant. Juggling seven online courses with limited resources meant something always had to give, and usually it was my engagement with the harder material.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the course page open, scrolling through, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. Anxiety does that—you're physically present but mentally spiraling about everything that could go wrong. I'd watch videos without processing a single word. Open readings and stare at them. The activity was there, but the learning wasn't.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially assignments that scared me. The bigger and more important something seemed, the more I'd avoid it. It's not logical—I knew procrastinating made everything worse. But the fear of failing was so intense that not trying felt safer than trying and confirming I couldn't do it. I'd distract myself until the deadline forced my hand.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They were the only thing that could override my avoidance. I'd barely touch the LMS for weeks, then suddenly be on there constantly as a deadline approached. My engagement was basically a series of panic spikes rather than steady involvement. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything at all. They were terrifying but necessary.
Q17: Feedback was complicated. Good feedback motivated me for maybe a day before the anxiety crept back. Critical feedback, even constructive stuff, would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course entirely for a while. I wanted feedback but also dreaded it. When I did well on something, it almost felt like a fluke, so it didn't build lasting confidence.
Q18: The structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to cling to when everything felt chaotic. But seven courses meant the structures overlapped and competed. I couldn't keep track of everything. A simpler, more linear structure might have helped me stay on track instead of constantly playing catch-up across multiple courses.
Q19: The reading-heavy approach worked for me since that's my learning style. But the sheer volume of content was paralyzing. When I'd fall behind, catching up seemed impossible, which made me avoid it more. Smaller, more digestible chunks would have helped. Also, the discussion requirements were good for forcing engagement, but sometimes they felt performative rather than meaningful.
Q20: That's basically me. High activity because I was constantly checking things out of anxiety, but low submission because actually completing work felt too risky. You can spend hours in the LMS without producing anything if you're stuck in an anxiety loop. Clicking around, reading the same things repeatedly, trying to work up the courage to start an assignment. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: I can see that too. Some people are just efficient—they know what they need, they get it, they leave. I watched people in discussions who barely posted but clearly understood everything. If you study effectively offline or already have background knowledge, you don't need to live in the LMS. My decent exam scores came partly from cramming with offline materials, not from my scattered LMS activity.
Q22: Logs can show when you're present and what you clicked on. They can track patterns like deadline-driven spikes. But they can't show understanding, fear, genuine engagement versus anxious scrolling. They miss offline work entirely. They can't capture that I might spend three hours with a downloaded reading or that my high login frequency was driven by panic rather than enthusiasm.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, when you want to understand rather than just pass. For me, real engagement was rare—mostly I was just surviving, trying to check boxes while managing constant anxiety. True engagement would mean learning without the fear driving everything.
Q24: The fear. The constant, exhausting fear that I'm not good enough, that I'm going to fail, that everyone else has it figured out except me. The LMS can't show that I did the mindfulness exercises and they actually helped a little. It can't show the offline studying, the financial stress, the way I'd stare at an assignment for hours before giving up. It shows a shadow of my experience, not the reality.
